From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Reference frame for European geodesy

This article is about the geodetic datum. For ED-50 in pharmacology, see [Effective dose (pharmacology)](/wiki/Effective_dose_(pharmacology) "Effective dose (pharmacology)").

**ED50** ("**European Datum 1950**", **EPSG:4230**[[1]](#cite_note-EPSG-1)) is a [geodetic datum](/wiki/Datum_(geodesy) "Datum (geodesy)") which was defined after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") for the international connection of [geodetic networks](/wiki/Geodetic_network "Geodetic network").

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ED50&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Kriegsweihnacht_1940-Hemmerich.jpg/250px-Kriegsweihnacht_1940-Hemmerich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kriegsweihnacht_1940-Hemmerich.jpg)

Gerlach Hemmerich

Some of the important battles of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") were fought on the borders of [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") and [France](/wiki/France "France"), and the mapping of these countries had incompatible [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") and [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") positioning. During the war the German Military Survey (Reichsamt Kriegskarten und Vermessungswesen), under the command of Lieutenant General Gerlach Hemmerich, began systematically mapping the portion of Europe under [German](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") military control.[[2]](#cite_note-Kettle1-2) [The Allies](/wiki/The_Allies_(WWII) "The Allies (WWII)") were also concerned about the state of mapping in Europe, and in 1944 the [US Army Map Service](/wiki/Army_Map_Service "Army Map Service") set up an intelligence team to collect mapping and surveying information from the Germans as the allied armies moved through Europe after the [Normandy landings](/wiki/Normandy_landings "Normandy landings"). The group, known as Houghteam after Major Floyd W. Hough, collected much material. Their greatest success was in April 1945, when they found the entire geodetic archives of the German Army cached in [Saalfeld](/wiki/Saalfeld "Saalfeld"), [Thuringia](/wiki/Thuringia "Thuringia"). The shipment, 75 truckloads in all, was transferred to [Bamberg](/wiki/Bamberg "Bamberg"), and then to [Washington](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") for evaluation.[[3]](#cite_note-Kettle2-3)

Shortly afterward, the team captured the personnel of the Reichsamt für Landesaufnahme, the State Surveying Service, in [Friedrichroda](/wiki/Friedrichroda "Friedrichroda"), Thuringia. This group had been working under renowned [geodesist](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") Professor Erwin Gigas to integrate the mapping of the occupied territories with that of Germany. They were directed to continue this work in US-occupied Bamberg, as part of the US-led effort to develop a single adjusted [triangulation](/wiki/Triangulation "Triangulation") for [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe"). This was completed in 1947.[[4]](#cite_note-Kettle3-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Hough1947-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Hough1948-6) The work was then extended to cover much of [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe") which was completed in 1950, and became ED50.[[4]](#cite_note-Kettle3-4)

The European triangulation was originally classified military information. It was declassified in the 1960s, and became the basis for the definition of international boundaries in the North Sea.[[7]](#cite_note-7) It remains used in much of Western Europe apart from [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"), [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), which have their own datums.

ED50 used the [International Ellipsoid](/wiki/International_Ellipsoid "International Ellipsoid") of 1924 ("[Hayford](/wiki/John_Fillmore_Hayford "John Fillmore Hayford")-Ellipsoid" of 1909) ([radius](/wiki/Earth_radius "Earth radius") of the Earth's [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") 6378.388 km, [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") 1/297, both exact). The spheroid was an early attempt to model the entire Earth and was widely used around the world until the 1980s, when [GRS 80](/wiki/GRS_80 "GRS 80") and [WGS 84](/wiki/WGS_84 "WGS 84") were established.

Many national coordinate systems of [Gauss–Krüger](/wiki/Gauss%E2%80%93Kr%C3%BCger_coordinate_system "Gauss–Krüger coordinate system") are defined by ED50 and oriented by means of [geodetic astronomy](/wiki/Geodetic_astronomy "Geodetic astronomy"). Up to now it has been used in [databases](/wiki/Database "Database") of gravity fields, [cadastre](/wiki/Cadastre "Cadastre"), small [surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") networks in Europe and America, and by some developing countries with no modern baselines. ED50 was also part of the fundamentals of the [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") coordinates ([Gauss–Krüger](/wiki/Gauss%E2%80%93Kr%C3%BCger_coordinate_system "Gauss–Krüger coordinate system") and [UTM](/wiki/Universal_Transverse_Mercator_coordinate_system "Universal Transverse Mercator coordinate system")) up to the 1980s.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The geodetic datum of **ED50** was centred at the Helmertturm on the [Telegrafenberg](/wiki/Telegrafenberg "Telegrafenberg") in [Potsdam](/wiki/Potsdam "Potsdam"), [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") with the intent to encourage cooperation with European socialist states during the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"). The adjustments for later versions of the datum ([ED77](/w/index.php?title=ED77&action=edit&redlink=1 "ED77 (page does not exist)"), [ED79](/w/index.php?title=ED79&action=edit&redlink=1 "ED79 (page does not exist)")) used the [Munich Frauenkirche](/wiki/Munich_Frauenkirche "Munich Frauenkirche") as starting point.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Datum shift between ED50 and WGS 84

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ED50&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Datum shift between ED50 and WGS 84")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/ED50 "Special:EditPage/ED50") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(January 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The longitude and latitude lines on the two datums are the same in the [Arkhangelsk Oblast](/wiki/Arkhangelsk_Oblast "Arkhangelsk Oblast") of north-west [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"). As one moves westwards across Europe, the longitude lines on ED50 gradually become further west than their WGS 84 equivalents, and are around 100 metres west in [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") and [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"). Moving southwards, the latitude lines on ED50 gradually become further south than the WGS 84 lines, and are around 100 m south in the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea").

This means that if the lines are further west, the longitude value of any given point becomes more easterly. Similarly, if the lines are south, the values become northerly. This is because the International Ellipsoid is larger than the GRS 80/WGS 84 ellipsoid, so a given north-south distance will cover more latitude on the new ellipsoid, and a given east-west distance will cover more longitude.

The datum shift for the [Universal Transverse Mercator](/wiki/Universal_Transverse_Mercator "Universal Transverse Mercator") grid is different. The eastings vary between 0 m in Eastern Europe and 100 m in the far west of the continent, roughly similar to the longitude shift, but the northings are about 200 m different across Europe, with the ED50 UTM northing lines south of the WGS 84 UTM northing lines. UTM northings are measured from the Equator; distance from the equator to latitude 50 degrees is 112 m more on the International than on the WGS 84 spheroid.
